coredata.dc:title: Enhanced magnetic hyperthermia of green-synthesized CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites utilizing moringa oleifera extract and watermelon peels

coredata.dc:description: This research focuses on cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4)/carbon dots (C-dots) nanocomposites synthesized through green synthesis, utilizing Moringa oleifera leaf extract and watermelon rind waste for magnetic hyperthermia applications in cancer therapy, enabling targeted and localized heating. CoFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized via a co-precipitation method, while the C-dots were produced hydrothermal. Sonication was applied to connect the C-dots to the surface of CoFe2O4. The X-ray diffraction spectrum of CoFe2O4/C-dots shows a cubic spinel structure with crystallite sizes ranging from 7.6 nm to 6.2 nm, where the crystallite size decreases with increasing C-dots concentration. Transmission electron microscopy observations reveal that the size of the nanocomposites is almost consistent at around 11.8–12.1 nm. The Co–O and Fe–O functional groups detected in the nanocomposite indicate that CoFe2O4 remains after fabrication. The identified C–O, CC, and O–H groups also confirm the presence of C-dots on the CoFe2O4 surface. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum peaks at 282 nm for C-dots and 218 nm for CoFe2O4. The addition of C-dots affects the saturation magnetization and domain size, which are 55–56.4 emu/g and 3.2–3.5 nm, indicating good magnetic properties. Additionally, the specific absorption rate (SAR) value of CoFe2O4/C-dots depends on magnetization, with the SAR value decreasing with increasing C-dots concentration while increasing with higher frequency and alternating magnetic field. The highest SAR values for CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots are 626 mW/g and 485 mW/g, respectively, which decrease with an increase in C-dots concentration. These results indicate that CoFe2O4/C-dots synthesized using green methods are promising candidates that can optimize performance in magnetic hyperthermia applications in the future.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the step-by-step synthesis process for CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites: (a) preparation of MO extract solution, (b) green synthesis of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles using the co-precipitation method, (c) hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots using watermelon peel waste, and (d) fabrication of CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites through the self-assembly of C-dots onto the surface of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic of Calorimetric heating measurement of the CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites.
Step-by-step mechanism of CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposite formation.
XRD patterns of (a) CoFe2O4, (b) CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL, (c) CoFe2O4/C-dots 15 mL, (d) CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL, (e) CoFe2O4/C-dots 25 mL, and (f) CoFe2O4/C-dots 30 mL nanocomposites.
TEM micrographs with HRTEM of (a) CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL and (c) CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL, particle size distributions of (b) CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL and (d) CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL.
FTIR spectra of (a) C-dots, (b) CoFe2O4 nanoparticles, (c) CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL, (d) CoFe2O4/C-dots 15 mL, (e) CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL (f) CoFe2O4/C-dots 25 mL, and (g) CoFe2O4/C-dots 30 mL.
UV–Vis spectra of C-dots, CoFe2O4, and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposite particles with various C-dots concentrations.
(a) Magnetic hysteresis (M − H) curve at room temperature and (b) The switching field distribution (SFD), room temperature of dM/dH versus H for CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites.

Induction heating curves of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dot nanoparticles at various concentrations under different conditions: (a) 10 kHz, 100 Oe; (b) 10 kHz, 150 Oe; (c) 15 kHz, 100 Oe; (d) 15 kHz, 150 Oe; (e) 20 kHz, 100 Oe; and (f) 20 kHz, 150 Oe.
SAR values of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dot nanocomposites at various concentrations under (a) 100 Oe and (b) 150 Oe magnetic fields.
Heat generation mechanisms by CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots MNPs.
Crystallite size and lattice parameters of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites with varying C-dot concentrations.

head.ce:title: Enhanced magnetic hyperthermia of green-synthesized CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites utilizing moringa oleifera extract and watermelon peels

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research focuses on cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4)/carbon dots (C-dots) nanocomposites synthesized through green synthesis, utilizing Moringa oleifera leaf extract and watermelon rind waste for magnetic hyperthermia applications in cancer therapy, enabling targeted and localized heating. CoFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized via a co-precipitation method, while the C-dots were produced hydrothermal. Sonication was applied to connect the C-dots to the surface of CoFe2O4. The X-ray diffraction spectrum of CoFe2O4/C-dots shows a cubic spinel structure with crystallite sizes ranging from 7.6 nm to 6.2 nm, where the crystallite size decreases with increasing C-dots concentration. Transmission electron microscopy observations reveal that the size of the nanocomposites is almost consistent at around 11.8–12.1 nm. The Co–O and Fe–O functional groups detected in the nanocomposite indicate that CoFe2O4 remains after fabrication. The identified C–O, CC, and O–H groups also confirm the presence of C-dots on the CoFe2O4 surface. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum peaks at 282 nm for C-dots and 218 nm for CoFe2O4. The addition of C-dots affects the saturation magnetization and domain size, which are 55–56.4 emu/g and 3.2–3.5 nm, indicating good magnetic properties. Additionally, the specific absorption rate (SAR) value of CoFe2O4/C-dots depends on magnetization, with the SAR value decreasing with increasing C-dots concentration while increasing with higher frequency and alternating magnetic field. The highest SAR values for CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots are 626 mW/g and 485 mW/g, respectively, which decrease with an increase in C-dots concentration. These results indicate that CoFe2O4/C-dots synthesized using green methods are promising candidates that can optimize performance in magnetic hyperthermia applications in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is known to be the leading cause of death worldwide [1]. According to data released by the World Health Organization (WHO) in 2024, new cancer cases in the world reached 20 million cases, with 9.7 million deaths. Cancer is a disease that occurs due to the uncontrolled growth of abnormal cells in the body [2]. Cancer develops cell signalling and apoptosis, making it a complex and intractable disease [3]. During the early 21st century, cancer treatment has been considered the most challenging health problem. Treatment of the main types of cancer includes radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and surgery [4]. Some of these treatment methods can cause many side effects and toxicity problems, so other alternatives are needed in the treatment of cancer [5].

Magnetic hyperthermia is known to have fewer side effects than existing therapeutic methods, such as chemotherapy and radiotherapy, so it is finally recognized as a promising method in cancer treatment techniques [6]. Its advantages in cancer treatment are its heat induction properties, chemical stability, and good targeting ability [7]. Magnetic hyperthermia is a therapeutic method that uses magnetic nanoparticles to increase the temperature in the tumor area, with the aim of damaging cancer cells [8]. Magnetic hyperthermia treatment aims to increase the temperature of the body area affected by cancer to 40–43 °C so that it can cause cancer cell death by increasing the cytotoxic effects of chemotherapy and radiotherapy [9]. Magnetic hyperthermia enables focused heating of cancerous tissue by using magnetic nanoparticles that can be directed to the cancer site via an external magnetic field, thereby reducing damage to surrounding healthy tissue [10]. The concept of magnetic hyperthermia utilizes the magnetic properties of nanoparticles that can be heated when exposed to an alternating magnetic field (AMF), which will undergo a change in magnetic orientation that produces heat [11]. The specific absorption rate (SAR), a metric representing the ratio of heat energy dissipation to the total mass of nanoparticles, characterizes the heat energy released in magnetic hyperthermia therapy. Several factors, such as particle size, saturation magnetization, magnetic anisotropy, frequency and strength of the applied external magnetic field, influence the SAR value. Generally, when the frequency and strength of the magnetic field are higher, the temperature generated is also higher, thus increasing the SAR value [12].

Magnetic nanoparticles, when exposed to AMF, will generate heat through the phenomenon of hysteresis heating, which allows a controlled increase in local temperature, and its advantages can be targeted specifically to the cancer area, which allows focused heat delivery, making it suitable for hyperthermia applications [13]. Previous studies have developed magnetic nanoparticles for magnetic hyperthermia applications in MnFe2O4 [14], such as CoFe2O4 [15]. One of the promising materials is cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) nanoparticles, which have an inverse spinel ferrite structure with collinear ferromagnetic properties derived from the antiparallel spin magnetic moment between Fe3+ ions in the tetrahedral A site and Co2+ in the octahedral B site, making it interesting to study because it has unique characteristics and potential in the biomedical field [16,17]. CoFe2O4 nanoparticles are widely developed in various fields, such as gas sensors, anti-cancer drug sensors and magnetic hyperthermia [18]. CoFe2O4 has superior magnetic properties, high magnetic permeability, good coercion coefficient and high saturation magnetization [9]. These properties make CoFe2O4 an ideal candidate for hyperthermia therapy, where local heating can stop cancer cell growth and damage tumor tissue without damaging surrounding healthy tissue [19].

A challenge faced in the use of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles is the issue of biocompatibility in biological systems. Combining cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) nanoparticles with carbon dots (C-dots) can improve heating efficiency and biocompatibility. C-dots are carbon nanoparticles that are 2–10 nm in size and have metallic characteristics based on quantum dots [20]. C-dots are also known as carbon-based nanoparticles that have unique optical and electronic properties and can serve as carrier agents to improve the distribution and stability of magnetic nanoparticles in biological media so that the combination of CoFe2O4 with C-dots can improve specific heating ability and provide better control of drug release in magnetic hyperthermia therapy applications [19,21]. In particular, the use of C-dots can bring dual benefits with high solubility in water, good chemical stability and good luminescence properties, making them effective coatings to enhance the luminescence features of magnetic hyperthermia [14,22,23]. In addition, CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites can utilize the synergy between the magnetic properties of CoFe2O4 and the luminescent properties of C-dots, allowing real-time monitoring of the distribution of nanoparticles in the body through imaging techniques. This is particularly important for clinical applications, where precise monitoring and control of the location and intensity of heating is necessary for therapeutic effectiveness [24].

Methods that can be used to synthesize magnetic nanoparticles include coprecipitation, microemulsion, hydrothermal, thermal decomposition, and sol-gel methods [25]. CoFe2O4 and C-dots nanoparticles can be synthesized through several methods, such as hydrothermal [26–28], and sonication [29]. Some previous studies still use conventional methods with many chemicals, which can potentially produce products that are less environmentally friendly or dangerous [30]. The problems of conventional methods can be overcome with a green synthesis approach that has the advantage of reducing toxic waste products, is safe for the environment, and is cost-efficient [31]. Green synthesis methods usually use plant extracts and microorganisms as bioreductors in nanoparticle synthesis [32]. Plant extracts are more than microorganisms because plant extracts do not require an incubation period, and the preparation is simpler, which makes the synthesis stage faster [33]. In addition, plant extracts also have phytochemical substances that can reduce metal ions so that they can be used in the synthesis of metal nanoparticles and metal oxides [34]. Plant extracts widely developed for the synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles are moringa oleifera and moringa leaf extract. Moringa oleifera (MO) is often utilized as a starting material for the synthesis of green nanoparticles because it contains a high amount of phytochemical substances, such as phenolics and flavonoids, which are known to have antioxidant and antimicrobial properties [35]. The green synthesis approach is also applied to preparing C-dots using fruit peels and waste materials as precursors [36]. Zhou et al. [37] reported the utilization of watermelon peel as a carbon source in the synthesis of C-dots due to its high organic carbon content, especially cellulose and pectin, which facilitates the carbonization process into small-sized C-dots (∼1.4 nm) through the hydrothermal method. C-dots from watermelon peel are also biocompatible and have good fluorescence ability, making them ideal for supporting theragnostic applications on CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites in magnetic hyperthermia therapy, with dual roles as heating and imaging agents [38].

As indicated by the literature review, Puspitarum et al. [39] utilized MO leaf extract as a reducing and capping agent in the green synthesis of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles. Larasati et al. [15] have reported the potential application of CoFe2O4/ZnS magnetic hyperthermia. Wang and Zhao [40] have reported C-dots/CoFe2O4 composites as separable visible light photocatalysts. However, no report has been found regarding the preparation of CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites by green synthesis using MO and watermelon rind extracts as magnetic hyperthermia.

This study synthesized CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites by green synthesis using MO extract and watermelon peels as magnetic hyperthermia. The utilization of magnetic and fluorescent properties in nanocomposites with surface modification of CoFe2O4 using C-dots can be used to evaluate the right dose and position in magnetic hyperthermia. The microstructural, optical, and magnetic properties of green-synthesized CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites were also investigated comprehensively. Additionally, the study explored the potential application of these nanocomposites in magnetic hyperthermia by varying the volume of C-dots incorporated into the nanocomposites. The variation of C-dots volume was carried out to obtain the optimum condition of CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposite that can show its potential application in magnetic hyperthermia.

The chemicals utilized in this study, including cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2⋅6H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3⋅6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), distilled water, and 96 % ethanol (CH3CH2OH), were acquired from Merck Chemicals (Darmstadt, Germany). The MO leaf extract was sourced from Timurasa, a local supplier, while the watermelon peel waste was obtained from a local market in Indonesia.

The MO solution was prepared by heating MO extract powder in deionized water at a 1:12 weight-to-volume ratio at 60 °C for 1 h with continuous stirring. Subsequently, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature and filtered using Whatman filter paper. The resulting MO solution was employed for subsequent synthesis, and the stock solution was stored in a refrigerator. This process is depicted in Fig. 1 (a).

The synthesis of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles was carried out through a straightforward and cost-effective co-precipitation method, following a previously reported procedure (Fig. 1(b)) [41]. This method utilized MO leaf extract as a reducing and stabilizing agent. Initially, cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2⋅6H2O) and iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3⋅6H2O) were dissolved in deionized water. Subsequently, the MO solution was gradually added under constant stirring to induce nucleation. Afterwards, a sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution was slowly added to increase the pH and trigger the precipitation of CoFe2O4 particles. The resulting precipitate was then separated, washed, dried, and calcined at a high temperature to obtain nanosized CoFe2O4 nanoparticles with desirable magnetic properties.

The green synthesis of C-dots was achieved using a hydrothermal method with watermelon peel waste as a carbon source. The advantages of watermelon peel over other organic wastes are its ability to produce C-dots with smaller particle sizes, good dispersion, and faster and more efficient synthesis reactions [42]. In addition, watermelon peel is an abundant, cheap, and environmentally friendly waste, thus supporting the principle of green synthesis while reducing organic waste [33]. The green synthesis of C-dots was illustrated in Fig. 1(c) [33]. Watermelon peel juice, distilled water, and 96 % ethanol were combined in a 1:1:1 ratio and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting solution was filtered at room temperature and stored for subsequent use.

The fabrication process for CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites is depicted in Fig. 1(d). To create these nanocomposites, 0.5 g of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles were mixed with 10 mL of C-dots solution. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min to ensure uniform dispersion of the C-dots. Over a 24-h incubation period, the C-dots spontaneously assembled onto the surface of the CoFe2O4 nanoparticles through a self-assembly process, as reported in the literature [43]. The resulting CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites were then magnetically separated from the solution, washed with distilled water to remove any impurities, and dried at 90 °C for 4 h to remove residual solvent. This procedure was repeated using varying volumes of C-dots solution (10 mL, 15 mL, 20 mL, 25 mL, and 30 mL) to produce CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites with different C-dot loadings. The variations are used to optimize CoFe2O4 particle size, maintain dispersion, luminescence, and improve heating efficiency in magnetic hyperthermia applications. However, C-dots are non-magnetic, so increasing their volume excessively will decrease the saturation magnetization (Ms) of CoFe2O4, which has a direct result on decreasing the SAR value [44,45]. Therefore, determining the volume variation of C-dots is important to maintain a balance between improving stability and dispersion with optimizing the magnetic properties of CoFe2O4 in magnetic hyperthermia applications. If the volume of C-dots exceeds 30 mL, CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites may experience a decrease in magnetic hyperthermia performance [33,46].

The synthesized samples were characterized using various techniques to investigate their structural, morphological, optical, and magnetic properties. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, using Cu-Kα radiation, was employed to determine the crystal structure and phase composition of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), including high-resolution TEM imaging, was used to study the samples' morphology, particle size, and crystal structure and visualize the atomic lattice arrangements. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed in the 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 range to identify the functional groups present in the samples. UV–visible spectroscopy was used to investigate the optical properties of the samples, including their absorbance and bandgap energy. Finally, the magnetic properties of the samples were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM).

A calorimetric method was employed to assess the magnetic hyperthermia properties of the synthesized nanoparticles and determine their specific absorption rate (SAR). A 0.05 g sample of nanoparticles was dispersed in 0.15 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 10 min to ensure uniform dispersion [47,48]. The resulting nanoparticle solution was then placed on an induction coil, and the temperature increase over time was recorded using a temperature sensor. The SAR values were calculated from the temperature increase data. To investigate the effect of magnetic field strength and frequency on the hyperthermia performance, the analysis was conducted at varying magnetic field strengths of 100 and 150 Oe and frequencies of 10, 15, and 20 kHz. A schematic representation of the calorimetric method used for magnetic hyperthermia analysis is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

A schematic illustration of the green synthesis process for CoFe2O4/C-dots is presented based on a literature review of existing mechanisms, as shown in Fig. 3 . MO leaf extract, rich in phytochemical compounds such as flavonoids, is a green reducing and stabilizing agent for synthesizing CoFe2O4 nanoparticles. Watermelon rind waste, a readily available and low-cost carbon source, is employed for the hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots. The flavonoid compounds present in MO extract, which possess hydroxyl groups, interact with Co2+ and Fe3+ ions, leading to their reduction and the formation of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles [49]. A nucleation phase commences after the reduction of Co2+ and Fe3+ ions, where these ions aggregate to form stable nuclei. These nuclei then serve as growth centres, attracting additional ions and growing into nanoparticles with well-defined shapes and sizes [49,50]. C-dots synthesis involves a green approach that utilizes biologically active compounds derived from watermelon rind waste to generate heat for the hydrothermal reaction. During the fabrication of CoFe2O4/C-dots, nucleation and growth processes occur, leading to the self-assembly of C-dots onto the surface of CoFe2O4 nanoparticles, resulting in the formation of the desired nanocomposite [44].

Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites with various concentrations examined using XRD analysis. The XRD pattern in Fig. 4(a) shows the diffraction peaks of CoFe2O4 observed at 2θ angles of 30.2°, 35.6°, 36.9°, 43.2°, 53.6°, 57.1°, 62.8°, 71.2°, and 74.6°, which correspond to Miller's main diffraction planes (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), and (533), respectively. Based on the CoFe2O4 database (COD No. 5910063), the sample has a cubic spinel structure with Fd3m space [51]. In addition, the XRD spectra of variation (10, 15, 20, 25 and 30) mL of CoFe2O4/C-dots show ten diffraction peaks, of which nine correspond to the cubic spinel structure of CoFe2O4. The diffraction peak of C-dots was identified at a 2θ angle of 18°, which is a small shift from the broad amorphous (002) peak observed at a 2θ angle of 19.18° for pure C-dots and is consistent with previous reports [52]. The peaks of carbon dots are usually amorphous or only show low crystallinity; Fig. 4(b–f) shows that there is a modification in the crystal structure of CoFe2O4/C-dots so that a peak appears in the (002) plane indicating the presence of C-dots on the surface of the CoFe2O4 nanocomposite [53].

The crystallite size was analyzed using the Gaussian function at the main peak (311) and calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation [54]. The crystallite size of CoFe2O4 was estimated to be 7.6 nm. In addition, the crystallite size is estimated to decrease to 6.3–6.6 nm for CoFe2O4/C-dots of various C-dots concentration variations of 10 mL–30 mL (see Table 1 ). Based on Bragg's law, the lattice parameters were obtained around 8.20–8.36 Å for CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots with various concentrations. These values are close to the standard data based on COD No. 5910063, which states a lattice parameter of 8.35 Å. Table 1 shows the crystallite size and lattice parameters of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites with various concentrations.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis was utilized to examine the morphology, particle size, and crystal structure of CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites with 10 mL and 20 mL C-dots concentrations. TEM images presented in Fig. 5 (a) and (c) demonstrate that the CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanoparticles are uniformly. The indistinct boundaries observed in the TEM images indicate that the C-dots effectively cover the surface of the CoFe2O4 nanoparticles [55]. In addition, the lattice parameters are also clearly visible when the HR-TEM images are enlarged; the measured d-spacings are about 0.28 nm and 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (220) plane and the (311) plane of the CoFe2O4 cubic spinel, respectively. This confirms the successful green synthesis of CoFe2O4/C-dots [40]. The particle size graphs in Fig. 5(b) and (d) result from normal curves fitting with the origin program. The average particle sizes of CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL and CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL were determined to be (12.1 ± 2.7) nm and (11.8 ± 2.4) nm, respectively. The particle size tends to decrease slightly as the concentration of C-dots increases, which can be attributed to strain-induced crystal distortion [56]. Similar findings have been reported in previous studies. A study by Latifa (2024) demonstrated that increasing the volume of C-dots from 20 mL to 30 mL during the synthesis of Fe3O4/C-dots resulted in a reduction in crystallite size from 7.1 nm to 5.8 nm [57]. Additionally, Sari reported TEM results showing that the average size of GS-Fe3O4 is approximately 14.6 nm, while the average size of GS-Fe3O4/C-dots is around 13.4 nm [33]. Similarly, a study of Sun (2018) reported that the addition of C-dots in the synthesis of magnetic nanocomposites led to a decrease in particle size from 10 to 20 nm for Fe3O4 to approximately 10 nm for the nanocomposite [44]. This slight reduction in particle size does not significantly affect efficiency in magnetic hyperthermia.

Fig. 6 presents the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra of C-dots, CoFe2O4, and CoFe2O4/C-dots. The presence of C-dots on the surface of CoFe2O4 is confirmed through the analysis of molecular bonds. CoFe2O4 can be identified through Co–O and Fe–O bond stretching vibrations in the 413-587 cm-1 [58]. These vibrations were detected in all variations of the CoFe2O4/C-dots sample, which experienced a relatively redshift. The incorporation of C-dots into CoFe2O4 led to the appearance of additional absorption peaks in the FTIR spectra of the nanocomposite samples. These additional peaks, observed at 973, 1622, and 3459 cm−1, can be attributed to C–O, CC, and O–H functional groups, respectively. The presence of the CC bond at 1622 cm−1 is particularly significant, as it is a characteristic vibrational mode of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the core of C-dots [38,44]. In addition to the CC bond, the FTIR spectra also reveal the presence of an O–H bond at 3311 cm−1, which indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the C-dots. These hydroxyl groups can contribute to the hydrophilic nature of the C-dots and facilitate their interaction with other molecules. In addition, there is a C–H bond around 2352 cm-1; when C-dots interact with CoFe2O4, C–H bonds can play a role in supporting the bond or interaction between C-dots and ferrite surfaces to improve the magnetic properties and catalytic activity of these nanocomposites [59]. The incorporation of different materials into nanocomposites can result in the formation of new chemical bonds or the modification of existing bonds between the constituent materials. These changes in the bonding structure can lead to the emergence of additional infrared absorption bands in the FTIR spectrum of the nanocomposite. The interaction between CoFe2O4 and C-dots in the CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposite can further modify the vibrational energy levels of the chemical bonds, resulting in a shift in the wave number of the corresponding infrared absorption bands [60].

Fig. 7 displays the absorption spectra of C-dots, CoFe2O4, and CoFe2O4/C-dots analyzed using UV–visible spectroscopy. The Cdots show a significant absorption peak at 282 nm, caused by
π → π
∗ the electronic transition in the aromatic CC bond, which is consistent with the FTIR results [61]. This indicates that Cdots derived from watermelon rind waste absorb UV light strongly, so they can potentially be used as photocatalysts under UV radiation. In contrast, CoFe2O4 and all variations of CoFe2O4/C-dots showed almost the same absorption spectra. CoFe2O4 has a maximum absorption peak around 218 nm and a broad shoulder peak between 365 and 400 nm [62,83]. Although the presence of C-dots in the absorption spectrum of CoFe2O4/C-dots is not very obvious, there is a small peak at around 220–233 nm, indicating carbon's presence [61].

Furthermore, the absorption spectra were used to calculate the band gap energy (E
g ) using the Tauc plot equation [62].

The absorption coefficient (
α
) is related to photon energy (
h v
), a constant (A), and an exponent (n) that depends on the type of electron transition (n = 2 for indirect, 3/2 for direct forbidden, and 1/2 for direct allowed transitions) [62]. The Tauc plot of the sample is shown in Fig. 7 which shows one of the E
g values of some nanocomposites whose values are determined. The Tauc plot for 20 mL CoFe2O4/C-dots is shown which can be obtained by plotting
(

against
α h
, then extrapolating the linear part of the curve to the x-axis [63]. The calculated band gap energies (E
g ) of the synthesized CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites are presented in Table 2 . It can be observed that the band gap energy of the nanocomposites increases with the increasing concentration of C-dots. This increase in band gap energy can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect, which occurs when the size of a material is reduced to the nanoscale. As the particle size decreases, the energy levels of the electrons become quantized, leading to an increase in band gap energy [64]. In smaller nanoparticles, the confinement of electrons within a reduced space leads to the overlapping of energy levels, resulting in narrower bandwidths and a larger band gap energy between the valence band (VB) and the conduction band (CB). The addition of C-dots to CoFe2O4 further increases the band gap energy of the nanocomposite. This is due to the difference in band gap values between the two materials. CoFe2O4 has a band gap ranging from 1.5 eV to 2.2 eV, depending on the synthesis method [65,66], while C-dots generally exhibit a higher band gap, ranging from 2.47 eV to 3.5 eV [64,67]. When C-dots with a larger band gap are introduced into CoFe2O4, an interface interaction occurs, modifying the electronic structure and causing charge transfer and redistribution of energy levels. This interaction leads to a shift in the Fermi level and an overall increase in the band gap energy of the nanocomposite. This phenomenon is supported by the study of Elkodous et al. [68] which reported that incorporating C-dots into a Co0·9Ni0·1Fe2O4/SiO2/TiO2 nanocomposite shifted the UV–Vis absorption peak from 365 nm to 299 nm, indicating a band gap increase due to electronic structure modification through interface interactions. The increase in band gap energy is consistent with the decrease in particle size observed in TEM and XRD analysis, as smaller particle sizes lead to stronger quantum confinement effects.

A Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) was tested with an external magnetic field ranging from 15 to −15 kOe. Fig. 8 (a) shows the magnetization hysteresis curves for CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL and CoFe2O4/C-dots 20 mL nanocomposites. The saturation magnetization (Ms) value for CoFe2O4/C-dots 10 mL was recorded at 56.4 emu/g, higher than the value of CoFe3O4/C-dots 20 mL recorded at 55 emu/g. The Ms value the higher concentration of C-dots is lower than that of the lower concentration. This lower Ms is due to the non-magnetic nature of C-dots, which inhibits the growth and movement of magnetic moments on the CoFe2O4 surface [69]. This decrease is likely due to stronger dipole-dipole interactions on nanoparticles with smaller interparticle spacing [70]. The small difference in Ms values between the two concentrations indicates that the material retains good magnetic properties despite adding carbon dots. This stability in magnetization suggests that this material should respond well in hyperthermia settings, allowing controlled heating of the tumor site without affecting surrounding healthy tissue [71,72]. Such high saturation magnetization also indicates a greater ability to respond to magnetic fields, which is crucial for effective heating [73,74]. In general, materials with Ms values above 40 emu/g can effectively convert magnetic energy into thermal energy, resulting in sufficient heating required for therapeutic applications [75,76].

Furthermore, the calculation of magnetic susceptibility (χ) uses the Langevin function, as seen from Equation (2) [77].

This curve depicts the magnetic field distribution required to change the magnetic moment direction of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) under normal space conditions. Fig. 9 (b) shows that the peak of CoFe2O4/C-dots is near (H = 0), suggesting a high initial magnetic susceptibility. This indicates that the material is easily magnetized due to its extremely small particle size and thermal energy. Based on calculations, the crystal sizes of 10 mL CoFe2O4/C-dots and 20 mL CoFe2O4/C-dots nanocomposites are 3.5 nm and 3.2 nm, respectively. Materials with good magnetic characteristics, saturation magnetization values, magnetic susceptibility and the right particle size (see Table 3 ) are very useful for magnetic hyperthermia therapy in cancer treatment [78].

Fig. 9 shows the temperature-time curves for CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots with different concentrations. The nanoparticles can increase the temperature by up to 14.4 °C within 10 min under a 150 Oe magnetic field. The temperature decreases with increasing C-dot concentration due to the reduced magnetic properties of CoFe2O4. The initial slope of the curves was analyzed using Origin software to calculate SAR values, were then calculated using the following equation (4) [79].

This research explores the influence of frequency, applied magnetic field strength, and magnetization on the specific absorption rate (SAR) of nanoparticles. Temperature measurements were taken at regular intervals over a 10-min period, with the magnetic field strength adjusted between (100 and 150) Oe and the frequency set between (10 and 20) kHz. These conditions were chosen to ensure safety and limit the high-frequency current to a maximum of 5 × 109 A/ms [80].

Fig. 9 displays a graph of temperature as a function of time with variations in AMF and frequency. In general, the SAR value is affected by several parameters, such as AMF strength and frequency, solvent viscosity, particle size, saturation magnetization, and magnetic anisotropy of the nanoparticles [81]. As shown in Fig. 9, higher AMF and frequency variations increase temperature over time. Conversely, increasing CD concentration results in smaller temperature increases. This is consistent with the VSM results, which show that non-magnetic C-dots decrease the saturation magnetization of CoFe2O4. As observed in TEM images, the lower SAR value is attributed to stronger inter-particle interactions caused by C-dot-induced agglomeration [62,82].

Fig. 10 demonstrates that an increase in C-dot concentration corresponds to a decrease in SAR value, while a larger variation in AMF and frequency results in a higher SAR value. These findings are supported by equation (3), which establishes a direct proportionality between the SAR value and the gradient of temperature rise concerning time.

To accurately assess the intrinsic heating ability of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) and to enable a more reliable comparison of SAR values obtained from different studies under varying external conditions, such as magnetic field strength, frequency, particle size, and MNP concentration, the more appropriate metric is intrinsic loss power (ILP), expressed in units of nanometers squared per kilogram (nHm2/kg). ILP is a normalized parameter that accounts for the specific properties of the MNPs and the applied magnetic field, providing a more accurate measure of their heating efficiency.
(5)

In this context, f and H denote the frequency and amplitude of the magnetic field, respectively. Table 4 presents the calculated intrinsic loss power (ILP) values for all samples. The ILP value for CoFe2O4 nanoparticles, determined to be 0.35 nHm2/kg, is consistent with previous research on their potential application in magnetic hyperthermia, which reported ILP values within the range of 0.35–0.68 nHm2/kg. These variations can be attributed to differences in synthesis methods and surface modifications [83].

Fig. 11 depicts the underlying mechanism responsible for the alteration in SAR values observed in CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots. The rapid temperature increase can be primarily attributed to Néel and Brownian relaxation processes induced by the applied magnetic field, with hysteresis loss contributing to the overall heat generation. Eddy current effects are negligible owing to the diminutive size of the nanoparticles. Néel relaxation involves the internal rotation of the magnetic moment within the crystal structure of the particle, whereas Brownian relaxation occurs when the particle rotates in alignment with the magnetic moment. The introduction of C-dots to the surface of CoFe2O4 introduces a more intricate Brownian relaxation process, resulting in a less pronounced temperature increase over time, particularly at elevated C-dot concentration [84]. Adding C-dots to the surface of CoFe2O4 results in a more complex Brownian relaxation process, which results in a slight change in temperature over time, especially at high C-dots concentrations. C-dots can help control the heating efficiency of magnetic materials in response to an alternating magnetic field (AMF). Through functionalization and surface modification, C-dots improve the stability and distribution of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) in biological medium, which results in better dispersion and controlled SAR values [85]. C-dots act as a stabilizer for MNPs by reducing clumping that can interfere with their magnetic response. By forming a stable composite, C-dots help maintain the dispersion and reactivity of MNPs under the influence of AMF, which is important for consistent heating during hyperthermia [86].

In the domain of nanomedicine, the integration of magnetic properties and biocompatibility is emerging as a significant aspect of cancer treatment applications. C-dots can significantly improve the biocompatibility of MNPs, reducing potential cytotoxic effects, which is important for medical applications such as hyperthermia therapy in cancer. The small size and surface characteristics of C-dots enhance the suitability of MNPs for biological environments, thereby increasing both patient safety and therapeutic effectiveness [86]. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of C-dots allow dual use for imaging and hyperthermia therapy simultaneously. This capability helps in visualizing the distribution of MNPs within the tissue, thus enabling real-time tracking during hyperthermia treatment [85].

Table 5 shows the results of previous research related to magnetite. The utilization of plant extracts, such as Moringa oleifera, in the synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles results in environmentally benign nanoparticles. Thus, this study offers a practical green method to produce multifunctional CoFe3O4/C-dots, which can be applied thus broadening the scope of potential applications for biocompatible nanomaterials in cancer therapy, encompassing both magnetic hyperthermia and drug delivery.

CoFe2O4/C-dot nanocomposites with varying C-dot concentrations were synthesized using coprecipitation and hydrothermal methods, employing eco-friendly MO and watermelon rind waste. XRD analysis confirmed reduced crystallite size after C-dot addition, while TEM imaging revealed fuzzy nanoparticles, indicating C-dot attachment to the CoFe2O4 surface. TEM analysis also shows that the particle size of these NPs is about 11.8–12.1 nm; High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis revealed distinct lattice parameters with measured d-spacings of approximately 0.28 nm and 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (220) and (311) planes of the cubic spinel phase of CoFe2O4, respectively. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis confirmed the presence of several functional groups, including Co–O, Fe–O, C–O, CC, and O–H, within the CoFe2O4/C-dot nanocomposites. UV–Visible spectroscopy analysis indicated a decrease in the maximum absorption peak of CoFe2O4 as the concentration of C-dots increased. Vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) analysis revealed a reduction in saturation magnetization (Ms) and domain size upon the addition of non-magnetic C-dots. The specific absorption rate (SAR) value of CoFe2O4/C-dots decreased with increasing C-dot concentration but increased with higher frequency and applied magnetic field (AMF) strength. These findings confirm the impact of Ms, frequency, and AMF strength on the SAR value of CoFe2O4/C-dots. CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/C-dots exhibited maximum SAR values of 626 mW/g and 485 mW/g, respectively, which diminished with an increase in C-dot concentration. This study underscores the potential application of these nanocomposites in magnetic hyperthermia for cancer treatment. The detailed information gleaned from this study provides valuable insights for researchers to further explore the development of effective nanocomposite magnetic materials for magnetic hyperthermia.
